summary: Gruber critiques the premature declarations of victory for Windows 8 as a tablet OS, particularly Paul Thurrott's tweet claiming the iPad should concede defeat. He notes that Microsoft showed an original Metro-style interface running on high-powered Intel PC hardware, not actual tablet hardware, with the OS still a year from release. Gruber contrasts Apple's approach of only revealing finished products with Microsoft's strategy of showing half-finished software on prototype hardware. He argues that declaring victory based on a demo running on non-representative hardware, a year before shipping, against an already phenomenally successful iPad is absurd. He points to Amazon's silence-until-ready approach as the more credible alternative.
key_points: Windows 8's demo ran on Core i5 Intel hardware with fans — nothing like the low-power mobile hardware actual tablets would need
Apple and Microsoft have fundamentally opposite product unveiling strategies: Apple shows only finished products, Microsoft gives early access to half-finished software on prototype hardware
Declaring Windows 8 the iPad killer based on a demo a year before release ignores that Apple will sell tens of millions more iPads before any Windows 8 tablet ships
The tech writers each company attracts reflect those companies' approaches — Microsoft writers embrace potential while Apple writers demand something real
Amazon's approach of saying and showing nothing until the product is ready is held up as the more credible model
money_quotes: It's all in the future. All potential, nothing actual.
I enjoy writing about what's real.
It's one thing to look at today's Windows-8-on-a-tablet and say that it has a lot of potential. It's another thing altogether to look at it and declare victory.
Show me something real, I say.
We've got 'is good today' against 'might be good in a year'. The actually good versus the potentially good.
The two companies have attracted the writers they deserve.
Who cares if it runs fast on high-power high-performance PC hardware, why can't we see it on low-power mobile hardware?
key_insight: Declaring victory based on a demo of unfinished software running on non-representative hardware, a year before release, against an already dominant product reveals a preference for potential over reality.
